zotero.abstractNote: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as components in complex systems, where they process inputs that contain both instructions and data. A common security concern is that an LLM might fail to distinguish between the two, leading to prompt injection attacks. In this work, we investigate the problem of instruction-data separation in LLMs. We first formalize the concept of instruction-data separation and propose a taxonomy of failure modes. We then introduce a benchmark for evaluating the ability of LLMs to separate instructions from data. Our experiments reveal that even state-of-the-art LLMs struggle with this task, and that the problem is more nuanced than previously thought. We conclude by discussing potential mitigation strategies and future research directions.
